1: Psalm 86 is the only Davidic psalm in Book III (Pss 73–89), which is otherwise dominated by Asaphite and Korahite compositions. Its placement here is probably deliberate: it forms a counterpoint of personal prayer amid the community laments of Book III. The psalm is one of the most extensively anthologized in the Psalter — nearly every phrase has a parallel in another psalm, making it a mosaic of earlier Psalter material assembled into a new personal prayer. "Poor and needy" (ʿānî wĕʾebyôn) — a hendiadys for those without social resources or legal protections; in the ANE legal context, the ʿānî (poor, lowly) and ʾebyôn (needy, destitute) were the specific objects of covenant protection and royal justice (Deut 15:11; Ps 72:12–13).
2: "Preserve my soul, for I am devoted to you" — šāmĕrāh napšî kî ḥāsîd ʾānî. Ḥāsîd (devoted one, faithful one, loyal one) is the term for one whose life is characterized by ḥesed — covenant loyalty. In the Psalter, the ḥăsîdîm are the covenant faithful who practice ḥesed toward both God and neighbor (Pss 4:3; 12:1; 16:10; 31:23; 37:28). The prayer appeals to the covenant as the ground for divine protection: the devotion the speaker has shown entitles him to the protection the covenant promises.
3: "Be gracious to me, O Lord, for I cry out to you all day long" — the appeal to divine grace (ḥannênî) appeals to the character of God rather than to any merit of the speaker. "All day long" (kol-hayyôm) emphasizes the persistence of the prayer — this is not a single petition but a sustained cry. The same continuous-prayer motif appears in Ps 55:17 (evening, morning, noon), reflecting the practice of multiple daily prayers as a discipline of covenant faithfulness.
4: "Gladden the soul of your servant, for to you, O Lord, I lift up my soul" — ʾeśśāʾ napšî (I lift up my soul) is the standard idiom for prayer orientation: the entire self directed toward God (Pss 25:1; 143:8). The request for joy (śammēaḥ) moves the prayer beyond deliverance to flourishing — the full wellbeing of the covenant life rather than mere survival. In the Psalter's theology, gladness (śimḥāh) in the divine presence is both a present experience (Ps 16:11) and an eschatological expectation (Ps 21:6).
5: "For you, O Lord, are good and forgiving, abounding in steadfast love for all who call on you" — ṭôb wĕsallāḥ wĕrab-ḥesed lĕkol-qorĕʾêkā. This is a compressed form of the Sinai self-revelation of Exodus 34:6–7. Sallāḥ (forgiving) appears as a divine attribute almost nowhere else in the OT — only here, Nehemiah 9:17, and Daniel 9:9, both explicitly reciting the Sinai formula. The phrase "for all who call on you (lĕkol-qorĕʾêkā)" universalizes the covenant attribute: divine ḥesed extends to all who pray, not merely to Israel.
6: "Give ear to my prayer, O LORD; listen to the voice of my plea for mercy" — taḥănûnî (my plea for mercy, my supplications) — plural, indicating sustained ongoing petition. In the later Second Temple period, this vocabulary of sustained supplication became the technical language for penitential prayer (Dan 9:3, 17–18, 20; Neh 1:6, 11; 1 Kgs 8:28–30, 38, 45, 49), linking personal distress to communal repentance.
7: "On the day of my trouble I call to you, for you answer me" — the confident assertion that YHWH answers prayer is built on accumulated covenant testimony: God heard in the past, therefore he will hear now. This is the same logic as the Exodus (recited as the ground for present petition in Pss 80:8–11; 81:10; 106:7–12; Neh 9:9–25).
8: "There is no one like you among the gods, O Lord, and no works compare to yours" — ʾên kāmôkā bāʾĕlōhîm ʾadonāy. The divine incomparability formula acknowledges the existence of other divine beings (ʾĕlōhîm) while asserting YHWH's absolute superiority. This is what scholars call "henotheism" — exclusive worship of YHWH alongside recognition that other divine powers exist but are incomparable. The same formula appears in Exod 15:11 ("Who is like you among the gods, O LORD?") and Ps 89:6–7 (the heavenly assembly context).
9: "All the nations you have made will come and bow before you, O Lord, and they will glorify your name" — the eschatological universalism of the Psalter: YHWH made all the nations, and the Creator's claim on his creation will ultimately be acknowledged. This forward-looking vision drives the NT's understanding of the Gentile mission as the fulfillment of Israel's worship vocation (Rom 15:9–12; Rev 15:4).
10: "For you are great and you do wonders; you alone are God" — ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm lĕbaddekā (you alone are God). "You alone" (lĕbaddekā) excludes all competitors absolutely — moving beyond the incomparability formula of v. 8 to exclusivity. The NT applies this exclusive formula to the Father (John 17:3; 1 Tim 1:17) and, through the Christ-confession, reshapes it as a trinitarian claim. The "wonders (niplāʾôt)" are specifically the extraordinary acts of salvation-history — exodus events — that no other god performed.
11: "Teach me your way, O LORD, that I may walk in your truth; give me an undivided heart to reverence your name" — lēb yāḥîd (an undivided/unified heart) is a unique phrase. The prayer is for interior integration: a heart not divided between YHWH and other loyalties. In the Shema's context (Deut 6:4–5: "Love the LORD your God with all your heart"), the "whole heart" is the antithesis of the divided heart. Second Temple Judaism developed this into the concept of kavanah (directed intention in prayer).
12: "I will thank you, O Lord my God, with my whole heart; I will glorify your name forever" — the comprehensive thanksgiving vow follows from the "undivided heart" of v. 11: it is the whole heart that gives thanks. The Hebrew concept of "name" (šēm) encompasses the entire revealed character — to glorify the name is to publicly declare and honor the full character of YHWH.
13: "For your steadfast love toward me is great; you have rescued my soul from the lowest depths of Sheol" — šĕʾôl taḥtîyyāh (the lowest/deepest Sheol). Sheol in the OT is the underworld dwelling of the dead — not a place of punishment but of silence and separation from the divine presence (Ps 6:5; 30:9; 88:10–12). "Lowest Sheol" suggests the extreme end of the deathward direction; the divine rescue is thus a claim of exceptional salvation.
14: "O God, arrogant men have risen up against me; a band of ruthless men seeks my life — they do not keep you before them" — zēdîm (arrogant ones, the presumptuous) is a technical term in the wisdom tradition for those who presumptuously overstep covenantal boundaries (Pss 19:13; 119:21, 51, 69, 78, 85, 122; Prov 21:24). The defining characteristic: "they do not keep you before them" — the inverse of Ps 16:8 ("I have set the LORD always before me") — unaccountability before God produces violence toward humans.
15: "But you, O Lord, are a compassionate and gracious God, slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness" — ʾēl raḥûm wĕḥannûn ʾerek ʾappayim wĕrab-ḥesed weʾĕmet. This is the verbatim Sinai formula of Exodus 34:6 — the most foundational divine self-disclosure in the OT, repeated throughout Scripture as the bedrock description of YHWH's character (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2; Ps 103:8; 145:8). The contrast with v. 14 is stark: arrogant attackers vs. the compassionate God — the prayer draws on the divine character as the most reliable ground for rescue.
16: "Turn to me and be gracious to me; give your strength to your servant, and save the son of your maidservant" — ben-ʾāmatĕkā (son of your maidservant). A servant born into the household had a stronger loyalty claim than a hired or purchased servant — the "son of the maidservant" was fully the master's from birth, raised entirely within the household, not a newcomer who entered service by contract. Applied to the covenant speaker, it is a claim of lifelong belonging to YHWH's household. The same phrase appears in Ps 116:16.
17: "Show me a sign of your favor, so that those who hate me will see and be ashamed" — ʾôt lĕṭôbāh (a sign for good, a token of favor). The request for a visible divine sign serves a social function: the "sign" will be seen by opponents who will then be "ashamed (yēbōšû)." In ANE honor-shame culture, visible divine vindication restored the public honor of one who had been publicly attacked. The divine comfort (niḥamtānî) is not merely emotional relief but public validation.
